questions.question: Select all that are true about a converging two-pointer scan.
choices: It requires the array to be sorted.
One pointer starts on the smallest element and the other on the largest.
It works equally well on an unsorted array.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The converging walk needs a sorted array so a single comparison can discard a whole end. One pointer sits on the smallest value, one on the largest.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the correct move when a converging pair sum is smaller than the target.
choices: Advance the left pointer toward a bigger value.
Retreat the right pointer toward a smaller value.
Move both pointers inward at the same time.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A too-small sum means the smallest value cannot help, so the only way to raise the sum is to advance the left pointer toward a bigger value.

questions.question: Select all that are NOT true about why sorted order makes the pair-sum comparison decisive.
choices: A sum that is too large condemns every pair using the current largest element at once.
One comparison rules out an entire class of candidate pairs, not just one pair.
On unsorted data, moving a pointer still reliably pushes the sum in the expected direction.
Sorted order is irrelevant to whether a comparison is decisive.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The first two statements are the true decisive-comparison argument. On unsorted data moving a pointer does not reliably push the sum, and sorted order is exactly the license for the walk, so those two false statements are the NOT-true selections.

questions.question: Select all that are true about how to solve two-sum on an UNSORTED array that must return the original indices.
choices: A converging two-pointer scan from both ends solves it directly.
Sorting the array first and then using two pointers preserves the original indices for free.
The same-direction read/write compaction solves it.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Unsorted two-sum lacks the decisive comparison, and sorting destroys the original indices. The taught fix is a hash map of complements, which is not listed, so None of the above is correct.

questions.question: Select all that are true about the read and write pointers in an in-place dedup of a sorted array.
choices: The read pointer marches across every element, inspecting each one.
Everything strictly to the left of the write pointer is the finished, compacted answer.
The write pointer advances only when the read pointer finds a new value worth keeping.
None of the above
questions.explanation: Read scouts every element, the region left of write is the finished answer (the write boundary invariant), and write advances only on a keeper.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why 'container with most water' always moves the pointer at the shorter wall.
choices: The shorter wall caps the area, so moving the taller wall can only shrink the width without raising the cap.
The taller wall is always on the left.
Moving the shorter wall keeps the array sorted.
None of the above
questions.explanation: The shorter wall is the limiting factor, so only abandoning it gives a chance at a taller cap. Moving the taller wall just loses width.

questions.question: Select all that are true about why Floyd's fast and slow pointers collide inside a cycle.
choices: On a finite loop the gap between them shrinks by one each tick, so it must reach zero.
The fast pointer repeatedly restarts from the head of the list.
They collide only when the loop length is even.
None of the above
questions.explanation: A steadily closing gap of one per tick on a finite circular path forces a meeting. The fast pointer does not restart, and the loop length parity is irrelevant.

questions.question: Select all that are true about read/write recognition cues and how a window relates to two pointers.
choices: 'in place' is a fingerprint of the same-direction read/write compaction.
'O(1) extra space' is a fingerprint of the same-direction read/write compaction.
A sliding window is a converging two-pointer setup that starts from opposite ends.
None of the above
questions.explanation: 'in place' and 'O(1) extra space' both fingerprint the compaction. A sliding window is a same-direction two-pointer setup where you care about the span between the pointers, not a converging one.
